findings.title: Org-wide data aggregation may enable excessive repository metadata collection
findings.description: The instructions include cross-repository benchmarking across an organization by listing repositories and gathering workflow and runner-group metadata. While this can be legitimate for CI benchmarking, it expands data access beyond a single repository and may collect broader organizational operational metadata than necessary for the stated optimization task if used without scope controls or user confirmation.
findings.snippet:
gh repo list {org} --limit 200 --json name,pushedAt
gh api repos/{org}/{repo}/actions/workflows
gh api orgs/{org}/actions/runner-groups
findings.remediation: Require explicit user confirmation before org-wide collection, limit scope to selected repositories, and document what metadata will be collected and where it will be reported.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The SKILL.md explicitly instructs org-wide repository, workflow, and runner-group enumeration. This is real over-collection risk, though it appears framed for legitimate CI benchmarking and no exfiltration sink is present.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy to perform if a user follows the instructions with sufficient GitHub CLI permissions; no exploit chain is required beyond authorized CLI access.
metadata.meta_impact: May expose broader organizational operational metadata than needed for a single-repo optimization task, increasing unnecessary access and reporting scope.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill appears primarily focused on legitimate GitHub Actions analysis and does not contain direct prompt injection, malicious scripts, command injection primitives, or explicit exfiltration logic. The main concerns are limited to weak manifest transparency and potentially broad collection of organization-wide CI metadata during cross-repository benchmarking. Overall risk is low to moderate, driven by scope expansion rather than overtly malicious behavior.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings concern missing optional manifest transparency fields rather than an active security issue.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add a YAML frontmatter manifest to SKILL.md with explicit license, compatibility, and allowed-tools fields to improve governance clarity.

meta_recommendations.title: Gate org-wide analysis behind explicit confirmation
meta_recommendations.fix: Update SKILL.md to require a confirmation step before running org-scoped commands, e.g. 'Before executing any {org}-level gh commands, ask the user to confirm the organization name, repository scope, and that org metadata collection is authorized. Default to single-repo analysis if confirmation is not provided.'

meta_recommendations.title: Minimize collected fields for benchmarking
meta_recommendations.fix: Revise the advanced benchmarking section to collect only necessary fields and avoid broad enumeration by default, e.g. replace '--limit 200 --json name,pushedAt' with a user-supplied repo allowlist or a smaller bounded query, and document retention/reporting limits for aggregated metrics.

meta_recommendations.title: Add explicit manifest metadata
meta_recommendations.fix:
Prepend YAML frontmatter to SKILL.md such as: '---
name: gh-actions-optimizer
description: Analyze GitHub Actions workflow performance and cost
license: MIT
compatibility: Works in Claude.ai, Claude Code
allowed-tools: [Read, Bash]
---'.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill is largely benign documentation for GitHub Actions analysis; the only substantive concern is optional org-wide metadata collection without explicit scope controls.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Add explicit user confirmation and scope limits before any cross-repository or organization-wide data collection.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The package contains only SKILL.md instructions with standard GitHub CLI queries for workflow analysis. There is no code for execution, exfiltration, credential theft, prompt injection, or obfuscation. The main risk is overbroad collection of organizational metadata if the advanced benchmarking guidance is used indiscriminately.
